coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots modified Z and S–Scheme heterojunctions for pharmaceutical contaminants photodegradation: State–of–the–art, benefits, and limitations

coredata.dc:description: In the last few years, the amount of wastewater–containing pharmaceutical contaminants that are released into the environment from hospitals, pharmaceutical industry, and aquaculture sector has significantly increased. In fact, it has become a real issue due to the excessive uses of pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs), fast urbanization, medical sector development, and the progress in the anthropogenic activities which directly polluted water. In this context, many efforts have been made in order to effectively remediate wastewater–containing pharmaceutical contaminants employing several processes (i.e., adsorption, biological remediation, membrane separation, electrochemical treatments, photocatalysis, flocculation, coagulation processes, etc.). Even though the mentioned processes present numerous benefits in the remediation of wastewater–containing active pharmaceutical ingredients, efficient feedstocks must be developed to achieve sustainability goals in wastewater remediation purposes. In order to attain such objectives, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) nanomaterials have attracted the attention of scientists and industrialists in pharmaceutical contaminants degradation due to their stronger fluorescence with high quantum yields in the low–energy region, synergetic effect, nontoxic, simple functionalization, tunable emission wavelength, etc. Herein, we review the recent developments made in CQDs–based Z/S–heterojunctions as high–efficient feedstocks for pharmaceutical contaminants degradation utilizing the photocatalysis process. The main sources of CQDs are also described. The limitations and benefits of the studied process are outlined. In the future research work, new sources of CQDs must be manufactured and advanced processes should be applied in the field of wastewater–containing active pharmaceutical ingredients remediation employing CQDs as organic feedstocks which present a new trend in nano–materials synthesis and their usages for pharmaceutical pollutants abatement.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the last few years, the amount of wastewater–containing pharmaceutical contaminants that are released into the environment from hospitals, pharmaceutical industry, and aquaculture sector has significantly increased. In fact, it has become a real issue due to the excessive uses of pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs), fast urbanization, medical sector development, and the progress in the anthropogenic activities which directly polluted water. In this context, many efforts have been made in order to effectively remediate wastewater–containing pharmaceutical contaminants employing several processes (i.e., adsorption, biological remediation, membrane separation, electrochemical treatments, photocatalysis, flocculation, coagulation processes, etc.). Even though the mentioned processes present numerous benefits in the remediation of wastewater–containing active pharmaceutical ingredients, efficient feedstocks must be developed to achieve sustainability goals in wastewater remediation purposes. In order to attain such objectives, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) nanomaterials have attracted the attention of scientists and industrialists in pharmaceutical contaminants degradation due to their stronger fluorescence with high quantum yields in the low–energy region, synergetic effect, nontoxic, simple functionalization, tunable emission wavelength, etc. Herein, we review the recent developments made in CQDs–based Z/S–heterojunctions as high–efficient feedstocks for pharmaceutical contaminants degradation utilizing the photocatalysis process. The main sources of CQDs are also described. The limitations and benefits of the studied process are outlined. In the future research work, new sources of CQDs must be manufactured and advanced processes should be applied in the field of wastewater–containing active pharmaceutical ingredients remediation employing CQDs as organic feedstocks which present a new trend in nano–materials synthesis and their usages for pharmaceutical pollutants abatement.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The continuous increases of urbanization as well as the development of the industrial, cosmetics and medical sectors have led to the rise of the amount of water contamination [1–4]. Therefore, wastewater remediation has become a topic of great concern for researchers and industrialists because of the necessity of clean water for the humans’ life and the environment [5,6]. In fact, many organic and inorganic pollutants, such heavy metals, dyes, oil, pharmaceutical pollutants, radioactive substances, pesticides, etc., are widely detected in wastewater [7–12]. To deal with this issue, many works were conducted to separate these harmful organic and inorganic pollutants from degraded water and, therefore, to purify contaminated water applying numerous technologies including adsorption, photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, membrane separation, etc. [13–18]. However, in some cases, these conventional remediation approaches suffer from some drawbacks such as low–uptake capacity or selectivity, secondary pollution, high cost, and hard feasibility in real application for wastewater remediation purposes [19–21]. To date, natural organic and inorganic feedstocks from renewable and low–cost resources such as cellulose, chitin, chitosan, clay, biochar, coal fly ash, zeolites, etc., have shown promises in the most efficient removal of organic and inorganic pollutants compared to the traditional porous feedstocks in removal rate and selectivity towards these harmful pollutants [22–29]. Besides, these materials have attracted particular consideration thanks to their high surface area, versatile functionality, sustainability, biocompatibility and high efficiency in organic and inorganic pollutants removal [30,31]. Regarding water contamination by active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs), some reports have indicated that human development in medical sector and drugs production has raised the release of pharmaceutical compounds (i.e., aspirin, atorvastatin, azithromycin, metformin, trimethoprim, naproxen, acetaminophen, sulfamethoxazole, carbamazepine, tetracycline, diclofenac, metronidazole, ciprofloxacin, ampicillin, erythromycin, ibuprofen, etc.) in water, which has directly damaged soil, plants and also water (Fig. 1 ) [32–34]. According to a recent report published in the literature, the concentration of pharmaceutical pollutants in wastewater is relatively smaller than other pollutants, ranging from ng/L to μg/L [35]. Additionally, the World Health Organization (WHO) mentioned that the APIs resistance in nature is becoming a real issue and the remediation of wastewater–containing these pollutants should be dealt with urgently and the discharge of pharmaceutical contaminants into the environment without treatment must be seriously prevented [36].

It was reported that the most efficient methods for water remediation are advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) (i.e., photocatalysis, Fenton and photo–Fenton, photolysis, ozonolysis, electrocatalysis, thermal catalysis, etc.). In fact, these techniques could oxidize pharmaceutical pollutants into innocuous compounds (e.g., CO2, H2O, and carbon–centered radicals (i.e., R• or R•–OH etc.)) [37–39]. On the one hand, it was proven that nanomaterials (i.e., metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), 2D layers materials, carbonaceous nanomaterials and metals and metal oxide–based nancomposites) can serve as good platform for pharmaceutical pollutants removal from wastewater (Fig. 2 ) [40].

On the other hand, photocatalysis strategy is based on the manufacturing of powerful non-selective radical oxidants (e.g., •O2 –, •OH, 1O2, SO4 •–, etc.) employing visible light irradiation or UV photons on semi–conducting materials with sufficient amount of energy [40–42]. However, the major limitation of this process is the fast recombination of photoproduced electron–hole pairs (e −/h +) that considerably decrease the photocatalytic efficiency of the utilized photocatalysts [36]. It is worthy to note that numerous materials extracted from renewable resources have been utilized in wastewater–containing pharmaceutical micropollutants treatment employing photocatalysis process [43–45]. Nowadays, low–cost materials are still much needed in wastewater treatment technologies. Among them, CQDs which are fluorescent carbon–based materials with electronic transitions of π = π* (C = C) and n = π* (C = O/C = N) bands, low chemical inertness, exceptional optical properties, tunable luminescence, high stability, low–toxicity and excellent biocompatibility are considered as excellent candidates in wastewater treatment [46,47]. Since their discovery in 2004, they have shown great efficiency and economic benefits (i.e., low–cost and renewable materials) in several fields (i.e., biology, medicine, electronics, and environmental applications) [48–50]. Notably, they have been utilized as one of the most effective organic supports in the development of S/Z–scheme heterojunction photocatalysts employing several synthetic methodologies (i.e., microwave–assisted methods, laser ablation, electrochemical method, arc discharge strategy, pyrolysis, hydrothermal strategies, etc.) [51–53]. On the one hand, it was proven that Z–scheme heterojunction photocatalysts are excellent photocatalytic materials thanks to their chemical stability, separation of photogenerated carriers to produce active species, good light absorption and electron transport properties with high speed due to their quantum confinement. On the other hand, numerous studies in the literature have demonstrated that S–scheme heterojunction–based photocatalysts have an excellent photo–carrier redox capabilities, outstanding light absorption qualities and strong internal electric field that can efficiently degrade pharmaceutical micropollutants under visible light irradiation [54–58]. The objective of this review is to analyze the recent progress in the employment of photocatalysis process to treat wastewater–containing pharmaceutical pollutants employing CQDs decorated with Z/S–scheme heterojunctions. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, the topic is discussed for the first time. Furthermore, the main sources of CQDs are described. Then, the chemical routes and characterization techniques of CQDs–based Z/S–heterojunction photocatalysts are outlined. Finally, we highlight the current challenges and future perspectives of CQDs–based Z/S–heterojunction materials in photocatalysis process to prove the importance of incorporating Z/S–heterojunctions on CQDs surface for wastewater–containing pharmaceutical pollutants remediation. Although CQDs decorated with Z/S–scheme heterojunctions have shown high efficiency in pharmaceutical pollutants degradation, photocatalysis utilizing CQDs–based heterojunctions for these contaminants photodegradation still suffers from some drawbacks that should be addressed in the future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sources of CQDs and their applications
ce:section.ce:para: As far as known, CQDs are a 0D nanomaterials with size of <10 nm [59,60]. They are obtained from green sources including food wastes, renewable natural products, fruit, inexpensive biomass, chemicals, flowers, vegetables, etc. (Fig. 3 ) [59,60]. Furthermore, CQDs have been synthesized generally using heating in domestic microwave oven and hydrothermal strategy employing inexpensive equipment, one–step preparation, low energy consumption and simple manipulation [59,60]. For example, Da silva et al. developed low–cost synthesis of CQDs from the carbonaceous residues of Tahiti lemon (citrus latifolia) by fast and simple method. CQDs obtained in this study are highly fluorescent, with blue emission independent of the excitation wavelength, in addition to their good dispersion in water and photostability, the authors indicated that the obtained nanomaterials exhibited high relative thermal sensitivity of 2.69 % K−1 at 25 °C and can be utilized for heavy metals detection in wastewater [61]. In recent study, Yan et al. prepared a CQDs photosensitizer from turmeric natural plant as carbon source by solvothermal method. They indicated that the obtained CQDs possess photodynamic inactivation against bacteria in vitro system [62]. In 2022, Kaur and Verma reported a comprehensive review paper about fruit waste conversion into CQDs for bioimaging applications. They indicated that ∼931 million tonnes of food waste in the world can offer an excellent opportunity for the fabrication of CQDs with high quantum yield and multicolour emission. They concluded that it is crucial to pay attention to fruit waste composition, synthesis methods, biocompatibility, hydrophilicity, surface chemistry, defects, and surface states of CQDs. Finally, they concluded that CQDs obtained via bottom–up approaches (thermal, combustion, or microwave heating, etc.) have emerged as the most adaptable and valuable carbon nanomaterials in the field of cell imaging [63]. In another study, Raoul’s co–workers synthesized CQDs @silver nanohybrids using fruits and vegetable wastes (i.e., orange, mango peels and colocasia) via easy and green methods. They utilized the as fabricated CQDs–based materials as bio–larvicide against mosquito vectors (i.e., Aedes albopictus, Anopheles stephensi and Culex quinquefasciatus). They concluded that these nanomaterials can be effectively utilized to control mosquito breeding in sewage water, municipal garbages, stagnant water, etc. [64]. To sum up, CQDs–based materials from various resources can be utilized in various usages due to their unique proprieties, low–cost, biodegradability and high availability because of their diverse renewable sources.

Nowadays, it is well known that the photocatalysis process is a useful and green method for wastewater remediation technologies [65]. Additionally, this process is based on the redox capability of the utilized photocatalysts in the presence of light. Generally, semiconductors materials are utilized as photocatalysts for degrading organic contaminants to CO2, H2O and other small molecules. Compared to the different AOPs（i.e., Fenton oxidation process, sonochemical oxidation process, electrochemical oxidation process, sulfate radical–based advanced oxidation process, etc., photocatalysis process is a sustainable technology for wastewater treatment due to its advantages such as high mineralization efficiency of pollutants with minimal waste production, rapid oxidation rate, effectiveness, the flexibility of operation, and low–cost [65]. As new class of photocatalytic materials, CQDs contain carbon core inside and abundant functional groups on the external surface (i.e., epoxy, hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxyl groups) with light absorptionranging from 240 to 320 nm which can be excited by sunlight to produce photoinduced electron–hole pairs [66,67]. They displayed excellent optical behaviours and exhibited absorption, chemiluminescence, photoluminescence, and phosphorescence spectra [66,67]. Moreover, CQDs–based materials can act as electron donors and acceptors in either solution or suspension. CQDs have only sp2/ sp3–hybridized carbon framework and can be smoothly functionalized with various groups over their surfaces, further, offering exciting advantages for high functional photocatalytic materials synthesis. According to Yu et al. (2023), CQDs displays strong ultraviolet absorption ability with the band tail extending to the visible range. They showed how combining CQDs with semiconductor materials to create heterostructures with the right band arrangement can enhance interfacial charge transfer and prevent the recombination of electron-hole pairs. Additionally, the presence of several functional groups on CQDs surface makes them as excellent organic feedstocks with energy band regulation, local electric construction, multiple carrier activation and active site engineering for high photocatalysts synthesis in order to degrade pharmaceutical pollutants in aqueous environment [68] (Fig. 4 ).

Web of Science search results show that during the period from 2012 to 2023, there have been more than 10.000 articles in 2022 with CQDs as the keyword and more than 1.000 articles with CQDs for wastewater as the keyword at the same year according to the Web of Science. Besides, as depicted in Fig. 5 , the uses of these materials increased exponentially during this period and CQDs–based feedstocks are efficiently utilized in wastewater remediation technologies (Fig. 5).

It is worthy to note that CQDs–based materials have been extensively utilized in photocatalysis process especially in environmental applications (i.e., NOx removal, CO2 reduction, hydrogen–peroxide production, dyes degradation, water splitting, H2 production, membrane separation, etc.) and other applications including radical polymerization, bioimaging, biosensing, toxic metals detection, drug delivery, light–emitting diodes (CLEDs), etc. (Table 1 ) [47,69–85].

Since their discovery by Brad et al. in 1979, from the first to the fourth generation, Z-scheme heterojunctions were developed as effective ways to transport charges directly or indirectly, separating electrons and holes with high separation efficiency, robust redox ability, and broad light response range (Fig. 6 ) [86]. Thus, the charge separation Z–scheme heterojunctions help reducing recombination of the photogenerated carriers and enhances the overall efficiency of the employed photocatalytic materials. Additionally, the Z–scheme heterojunction architecture is one of several strategies employed to enhance the performance and efficiency of solar cells and light absorption, allowing for more effective degradation of organic pollutants in aqueous system. Consequently, photocatalysts based on Z–scheme heterojunctions have been manufactured as good feedstocks for pharmaceutical contaminants degradation in the last few years [87–89].

In this system, the photogenerated holes (h+) in the valence band of photocatalyst (I) react with electron donors (D), generating the electron acceptors (A). On the other hand, the photoproduced electrons in the conduction band of photocatalyst (II) react with A, resulting in D formation (Fig. 7 ). In the next step, the reserved photogenerated electrons (e-) in the conduction band of photocatalyst (I) and holes in the valence band of photocatalyst (II) are involved in the reduction and oxidation reactions, respectively. On the other hand, as depicted in Fig. 7, electrons from photocatalyst (I) are susceptible to be captured by the acceptor (A) because of their higher potential differences, which be considered as a greater driving force. Accordingly, holes from photocatalyst (II) can be effectively captured by the donor (D) [90–92].

In the S-scheme system, photocatalytic materials can be classified as oxidation photocatalysts (OPs) and reduction photocatalysts (RPs) (Fig. 8 ) [92]. In RPs, which have high conduction band, photogenerated electrons are efficacious, while photogenerated holes are ineffective and require to be removed by sacrificial agents. In comparison with oxidation photocatalysts, which are initially utilized in hazardous pollutants degradation, photogenerated holes are the contributed factors, while photogenerated electrons are useless. Additionally, S–scheme heterojunction photocatalysts are composed of RP and OP with overwhelmed band structures, which is coincident to type–II heterojunction photocatalysts but with a totally different charge–transfer pathway (Fig. 8b and c). In the type–II heterojunction photocatalysts, photogenerated electrons/hole pairs are assembled on the conduction band of OP and VB of RP, respectively, suggesting in weak redox ability for pollutants degradation. However, in S–scheme heterojunction photocatalysts, the strong photogenerated electrons and holes are reserved in the conduction band of reduction photocatalysts and valence band of oxidation photocatalysts, respectively, nevertheless the pointless photogenerated charge carriers are recombined, resulting in a strong redox potential [92].

Besides, the charge–transfer pathway in an S–scheme heterojunction photocatalysts can be explained as follows: In the first case, as presented in Fig. 9 a, reduction photocatalyst has higher conduction band and valence band positions and smaller work function relative to oxidation photocatalyst. Through the close contact of the two semiconductors, electrons in the RP spontaneously diffuse to OP, generating electron depletion layer and electron accumulation layer near the interface in reduction and oxidation photocatalysts, respectively. In this phase, the oxidation photocatalyst is negatively charged and reduction photocatalyst is positively charged. Simultaneously, an internal electric field directing from RP to OP is produced, as depicted in Fig. 9b. This internal electric field accelerates the photogenerated electrons transfer from OP to RP (Fig. 9c). Next, when oxidation photocatalyst and reduction photocatalyst come into contact, their corresponding Fermi energy should be aligned to the same level which leads to an upward and downward shift in the Fermi levels of OP and RP, respectively. In the third case, the photogenerated electrons in the CB of OP and holes in the VB of RP are inclined to recombine at the interface under the Coulombic attraction between holes and electrons [92]. Till now, due their high charge separation and excellent effectiveness, S–scheme heterojunction photocatalysts have been widely utilized for pharmaceutical pollutants degradation in wastewater [93–99].

CQDs is composed of sp2/sp3 hybrid conjugated carbon due to its carbon core structure. Therefore, it can be utilized as electron acceptors and donors (co–catalysts) [100]. Additionally, CQDs may occupy a certain band position when combined with semiconductor materials, which thermodynamically promotes the progress of photocatalytic degradation of pharmaceutical pollutants in aqueous solution [100]. For example, in 2019, Tang’s co–workers reported the fabrication of a biomass carbon quantum dot–Bi2WO6 hybrid photocatalyst with high performance for antibiotic degradation [101]. In this study, the author indicated that CQDs act as an electron acceptor to trap electrons from Bi2WO6 and inhibit the recombination of electron–hole pairs in the CQDs@Bi2WO6. Moreover, the conduction band of Bi2WO6 is 0.17 eV, which is higher than that of the O2/•O2– potential (–0.33 eV vs. NHE). Thus, the electrons in the conduction band of Bi2WO6 cannot react with O2 to form •O2–. However, the signal of •O2– can be detected in this performed study which demonstrates that the heterojunction formed between Bi2WO6 heterojunction and CQDs can be advantageous for the reduction of O2 to •O2– and further for antibiotics degradation [101]. It worthy to note recent studies have shown that CQDs can also act as semiconductors when graphite becomes carbon dots, in which CB and VB can be easily separated [102].

In this section, we described the findings associated with visible light and sunlight–induced photocatalysis employing some reported CQDs decorated with Z/S–scheme heterojunctions for pharmaceutical pollutants degradation. Besides, their chemical routes, characterization techniques and their efficiency towards pharmaceutical contaminants are also discussed. For instance, Ren et al. (2023) reported green and low–cost 0D/2D CQDs/Bi2WO6 S–scheme heterojunction photocatalyst with high quantum yield and lifetime by way of hydrothermal method (Fig. 10 ).

As displayed in Fig. 12 , they concluded the possible mechanism of TC photodegradation is because of the existence of •O2 –/•OH/h+ under visible light illumination which can be explained by the following equations (Eqs. (1)–(6):

In 2021, Adorna et al. reported indirect Z–scheme nitrogen–doped CQDs (Bi2MoO6/g–C3N4) for ciprofloxacin (CIP) photodegradation as model of pharmaceutical contaminant under visible light irradiation. The authors utilized FESEM, TEM, XRD, thermo–gravimetric analysis (TGA), FTIR, XPS, X–ray absorption near-edge structure (XANES), UV–vis–DRS, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) to confirm the successful synthesis of the Z–scheme photocatalyst [104]. They indicated that the graphitic structure of carbon nitride (CN) is defined by two broad peaks centering at 13.1 and 27.4°, which present the (100) and (002) interlayer diffraction of conjugated aromatic of g–C3N4 with the interplanar spacing of 0.344 nm. Besides, they indicated that there are four distinct peaks at 28.0, 32.4, 46.6, 55.3 and 58.6° are noticed, which can be attributed to the (131), (002), (062), (331) and (191) planes of Bi2MoO which confirm the good incorporation of Bi2MoO into g–C3N4. By way of XPS spectra, they indicated that the C1s core–level regions show four major peaks (i.e., C–N = C (288.2 eV) in C–N heterocycles, C–OH (286.9 eV), C = C in adventitious carbon (285.2 eV), and sp2 carbon (293.5 eV) in carbon nitride). Furthermore, the peaks appearing at 529 and 530 eV can be assigned to metal–oxygen bonds of Bi–O and Mo–O, respectively, while the peak at 531.7 eV is the main peak of the surface –OH bonding and in distinct spectrum of Bi2MoO6 at 157.8 and 163.1 eV can be attributed to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 peaks, respectively [104]. In XANES spectra, they demonstrated that the highest feature of electron yield at 289.8 eV is the C1s core exciton state because of the quantum confinement effect in NCD and CN. They concluded that the peak at 296.3 eV refers to the excitation of C1s to π* transition situated at C = O bonds of carboxylic acid and carbonyl groups. Moreover, the peak appear at 299.7 eV is due to the transition to π* states of C − O bond. The peak at 286.8 eV in NCD is attributed to the C1s → π* transition of graphene. Additionally, they demonstrated that for BMCN and NCD@BMCN feedstocks, the lowest energy peak appear at 285.5 eV is attributed to the transition of C1s into π* symmetry. During study, the authors utilized the as prepared heterojunction for ciprofloxacin degradation. They concluded that 63 and 73 % of CIP were photodegraded at pH = 5.0 and 6.0, respectively, within 30 min of irradiation time. They indicated that there is no obvious decrease in CIP concentration detected in the absence of the tested photocatalysts under visible light illumination, implying that the direct photolysis of CIP molecule is negligible. Besides, they demonstrated that the increase in pH values enhances the photodegradation rate of CIP molecule, and 90–98 % photodegradation efficiency of this pharmaceutical compound by NCD@BMCN is reached at pH ranging from 7.0 to 8.0 which can be considered as high degradation rate. Dang’s co–workers indicated that CIP is possibly degraded by the attack of oxygen–derived radicals such as •OH and •O2 – while hole (h+) only has a low contribution towards CIP photodegradation (Eqs. 7–11).

Based on the LC–MS results, the authors indicated that CIP photodegradation was performed through three pathways (piperizine ring breaking followed by cutting the CH3–NH bond, piperizine ring breaking and cleavage, piperazine breaking and defluorination) which led to the formation of small products like CO2, H2O and other low molecular compounds.

In another study, Xie’s group reported an environmental–friendly photocatalyst Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs Z–scheme heterojunction by way of hydrothermal–photoinduced method employing corncob charcoal residue as source of CQDs (Fig. 13 ) [105]. In order to evident the formation of heterojunction, the authors utilized FESEM, TEM, XRD, Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET), XPS, FTIR, UV–Vis–DRS, PL, inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP–OES), energy dispersive X–ray spectrum mapping (EDS–mapping), photoelectrochemical measurements, etc. In the performed study, they utilized the Z–scheme heterojunction photocatalyst for carbamazepine antibiotic degradation under visible light illumination.

They indicated that BiOBr exhibited thin sheet shape and magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles mainly displayed nanosphere morphology. In addition, Fe3O4 nanoparticles were well dispersed on the interface of BiOBr to form Fe3O4/BiOBr composite. Besides, they concluded that similar hierarchy morphologies were also detected on the ternary composites FeBr and FeBrCQDs–4, illustrating the successful loading of Fe3O4/BiOBr on the surface of CQDs. They indicated that many CQDs nanoparticles could be observed on the surface of BiOBr in the obtained FeBrCQDs photocatalyst. Besides, the lattice spacing of 0.28, 0.25 and 0.22 nm could be attributed to (110), (311) and (100) facets of BiOBr, Fe3O4, and CQDs, respectively, which prove the good combination of above three feedstocks. By way of XRD technique, they demonstrated that the diffraction peak intensity of FeBiOBrCQDs–4 was higher than that of FeBrCQDs–4 (no loading), suggesting FeBiOBrCQDs–4 fabricated by photoinduced strategy has higher crystallinity and better catalytic activity. Regarding carbamazepine photodegradation, the authors indicated that after the incorporation of Fe3O4/BiOBr on CQDs surface, the latter showed a good light absorption and electrons transfer abilities. Additionally, Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs performed the good photocatalytic activity, where the degradation rate of CBZ could reach high efficiency of 99.52 % within 120 min of contact time. They indicated that the electrons on the valence band of Fe3O4 and BiOBr were exited and transited to the conduction band meanwhile the photogenerated holes (h+) were created on the valence band (VB). Furthermore, CQDs materials as the receptor and donor of electrons favoured the transfer of electrons on the conduction band (CB) of BiOBr feedstock (ECB = – 0.26 eV) to the VB of Fe3O4 feedstock (EVB = 1.28 eV). Thus, the electrons on the CB of Fe3O4 material (ECB = – 0.33 eV) and the holes on the VB of BiOBr feedstock (EVB = 2.25 eV) could preserve good oxidation reducibility and enhance the catalytic activity of Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs feedstocks (Fig. 14 ).

Based on HRMS analysis, they indicated that three probable degradation pathways of CBZ molecule were provided. In the first pathway, •O2 – and h+ attacked heterocyclic olefin double bonds of CBZ pharmaceutical ingredient and oxidized it to generate product A with m/z of 253 under visible light irradiation. The product (B, m/z = 252) was produced because of the breaking of epoxy bonds which form product C (m/ z = 207) with the attack of photogenerated electron–hole pairs. Then, the obtained product C lost –C–O band to produce acridine D (m/z = 180), which could be then oxidized to form product E (m/z = 196) (Fig. 15 ). In the second pathway, •OH radicals could attack the heterorings of CBZ molecule to generate hydroxylated CBZ products F (m/z = 253) and G (m/z = 271), which were then further oxidized to form H (m/z = 267) through the ring contraction reaction, and the breaking of –CONH2 and aldehyde groups in product H could be converted into product E (m/z = 196) (Fig. 15). In the step III, CBZ molecules could be attacked by electron-hole pairs to form two following products (I, m/z = 224) and (J, m/z = 224). Subsequently, loss of aldehyde group and ketone could produce acridine (D, m/z = 180). In the presence of •O2 –, the by–product D could be oxidized to generate the intermediate E (m/z = 196). Then, the obtained by–products would further undergo redox and ring–breaking reactions to form the main ring-opening intermediates K (m/z = 137) and L (m/z = 115) under the action of reactive species, which were then mineralized into small products such as CO2 and H2O [105].

Overall, CQDs–based Z/S–heterojunctions are high photosensitizers of great interest to scientists and industrialists due to their unique optical, electrochemical properties, low-toxicity, wide–spectrum response, high–biocompatibility, stable photoluminescence and good quantum yield. In Table 2 , other examples of CQDs–based heterojunctions are stated to highlight the particular interests on CQDs renewable materials for highly efficient photocatalysts synthesis. Additionally, their decoration with Z/S–scheme heterojunctions and further their uses for pharmaceutical contaminants degradation are outlined [103,106–117].

XRD: X–ray diffraction, SEM: scanning electron microscope, EDS: energy–dispersive X–ray spectrum, TEM: transmission electron microscope, XPS: X–ray photoelectron spectrometer, FTIR: Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, UV–Vis–DRS: ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, BET: Brunauer–Emmett–Teller, UPS: ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy, EPR: electron paramagnetic resonance, EIS: electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, PL: photoluminescence emission spectra, ICP–OES: coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer, ESR: electron spin resonance, TC: tetracycline, CBZ: carbamazepine, OFL: ofloxacin, DCF: diclofenac, CIP: ciprofloxacin

The literature survey demonstrates that photocatalysis process is one of the effective and economic strategies employed to remove pharmaceutical ingredients from real and synthetic wastewater. For example, in 2023, Majumder et al. indicated that pharmaceutically active compounds (PhACs) such as sulfamethoxazole, 17β–estradiol, and carbamazepine, are ubiquitous in hospital effluent and are resistant to conventional treatment methods (Fig. 16 ). They mentioned that advanced oxidation techniques like photocatalysis can degrade these compounds. Majumder et al. utilized iron modified aluminum doped ZnO (Al ZnO/Fe) photocatalysts to simultaneously degrade sulfamethoxazole, carbamazepine, and 17β–estradiol. Batch studies indicated that at pH 8.0 and 0.5 g/L of photocatalyst, removal efficiencies of sulfamethoxazole, 17β–estradiol, and carbamazepine were 98.93 ± 0.27, 98.28 ± 0.35, and 98.31 ± 0.57 %, respectively. The toxicity of the intermediate products formed and potential application of the spent photocatalyst was also studied. However, removal of PhACs was decreased when the conditions were replicated in the continuous mode. Majumder’s co–workers demonstrated that their removal in tap water and real hospital wastewater was further reduced. The authors indicated that the presence of inhibiting agents in real wastewater attributed to the decrease in PhACs removal. In this context, a continuous baffled horizontal flow subsurface constructed wetland–based system followed by the photocatalytic reactor was employed to treat hospital wastewater spiked with sulfamethoxazole, 17β–estradiol, and carbamazepine, and the removal efficiencies were found to be 97.16 ± 1.85, 98.09 ± 0.92, and 96.02 ± 2.27 %, respectively [118].

Recently, CQDs as new class of carbon nanomaterials, have attracted considerable interest for applications in photocatalysis process due to their unique properties and high efficiency in pharmaceutical pollutants degradation [119–126]. Compared to other organic renewable feedstocks (i.e. chitin, chitosan, cellulose, lignin, biochar, etc.), CQDs based–heterojunction photocatalysts have their own benefits and limitations. The benefits of various heterojunction photocatalysts based on CQDs for pharmaceutical pollutants degradation are given as follows:
1.
The bandgap of CQDs can be tuned during synthesis by controlling their size, shape, dopants, and type of surface functional groups. This tunability allows for the absorption of light across a wide range of wavelengths, including ultraviolet, visible, and near–infrared light.
2.
CQDs typically have a high surface area to volume ratio, which provides more active sites for pharmaceutical pollutants photodegradation.
3.
Under light irradiation, CQDs can effectively generate electron–hole pairs. Efficient separation and transfer of these charge carriers to the pharmaceutical pollutants are crucial for their photodegradation.
4.
CQDs can act as both electron donors and acceptors, which means they can participate in various types of redox reactions. This dual capability is beneficial in degradation of pharmaceutical contaminants, where e−/h+ pairs interact with pharmaceutical contaminants leading to their breakdown.
5.
CQDs are relatively chemically inert and are resistant to photobleaching (loss of luminescence with exposure to light), which makes them suitable for long-term applications in photocatalysis especially for antibiotics degradation.

Despite their advantages, several limitations have been identified with the use of CQDs–based photocatalysts for pharmaceutical contaminants degradation, which are important to consider when developing practical applications of these pollutants treatment:
a.
One of the primary limitations of CQDs, in some cases, is their relatively lower quantum yield compared to other fluorescent materials like semiconductor quantum dots.
b.
CQDs are often heterogeneous in size, shape, edge structure, and surface functional groups. This heterogeneity can lead to inconsistencies in their optical and electronic properties, which makes it challenging to obtain highly efficient photocatalytic materials for pharmaceutical pollutants degradation.
c.
In some CQDs–based heterojunction photocatalysts, precise control over the synthesis of CQDs–based photosensitizers to ensure uniformity in size and properties is difficult and challenging. Additionally, the scalable production of high–quality CQDs is still a work in progress. This scalability issue makes it difficult to transition from academic research to industrial applications especially in the applications photocatalysis process for pharmaceutical pollutants degradation.
d.
Functionalizing the surface of CQDs to tailor them for pharmaceutical pollutants degradation can be complex. Besides, achieving a consistent coverage of functional groups without compromising the dots' optical properties is a technical challenge.
e.
The separation and purification of synthesized CQDs can be laborious. Additionally, establishing efficient and economical methods for their purification is necessary to isolate CQDs photocatalysts with the desired properties effectively.
f.
Despite some reported studies showing that CQDs photocatalysts are biocompatible and have low toxicity, systematic long–term studies are much needed to understand the potential toxicological impact, especially if they are utilized in pharmaceutical contaminants photodegradation.

The main goal of the CQDs decoration with Z/S–scheme heterojunctions is their potential for better charge separation and transfer, which can reduce recombination losses and improve the efficiency of CQDs–based photocatalysts for pharmaceutical ingredients degradation. Additionally, research works are continually trying to find the most suitable materials and configurations to build effective CQDs decorated with Z/S–scheme photocatalytic systems with high efficiencies and stability for practical pharmaceutical contaminants remediation. In addition to their outstanding performance to degrade pharmaceutical ingredients Z/S–scheme heterojunctions present some benefits and limitations which are stated bellow:
a)
Z–scheme heterojunction provides a direct pathway for minimizing electron–hole pairs recombination. Besides, this improved charge separation increases the efficiency of the photocatalytic pharmaceutical contaminants degradation.
b)
This system allows for more effective redox reactions which lead to carry out both reduction and oxidation reactions simultaneously and efficiently, which is essential for pharmaceutical pollutants degradation.
c)
Z–scheme heterojunction systems encompass wide range of materials (like metal oxides, sulfides, nitrides, etc.) with interface properties, resulting in good flexibility for specific pharmaceutical compounds photodegradation.

Like Z–scheme heterojunctions, S–scheme systems present some advantages which are given as follows:
a)
S–scheme heterojunction offers a stable charge transfer pathway, ensuring that the high reductive and oxidative abilities of the photogenerated charges are maintained long enough to participate in the redox reactions.
b)
The good separation of electron–hole pairs without significant recombination in S–scheme photocatalysts led to high contribution of the photogenerated charges in pharmaceutical contaminants degradation.
c)
With an effective selection of semiconductors materials, the S–scheme heterojunctions can absorb different portions of the solar spectrum, generating charge carriers effectively.
d)
The specific alignment of energy bands in S–scheme heterojunctions can enhance the stability of photocatalysts against photodegradation or photo–corrosion, which is a common problem in semiconductor–based photocatalysis.

To sum up, Z/S–scheme heterojunctions are an emerging approach to improve photocatalytic efficiency of CQDs nanomaterials. Attempts have been applied to use these systems for high efficient photocatalysts synthesis [127–131], with diverse sources of CQDs being explored to find optimal combinations of both materials for various pharmaceutical pollutants photodegradation. However, Z/S–scheme heterojunctions still suffer from some drawbacks which are mentioned below:
i.
Regarding Z–scheme heterojunctions, designing and constructing these systems can be complex, requiring precise control over the interface and the overall architecture. This complexity can be a barrier to large-scale manufacturing and commercialization and also for CQDs–based Z–scheme heterojunctions synthesis.
ii.
Finding two compatible semiconductors that can work in tandem to mimic the Z–scheme electron transfer process can be challenging. The semiconductors must have appropriate band alignments and sufficient chemical stability, which limits the choice of materials.
iii.
The efficiency of the Z–scheme heterojunctions heavily depend on the charge carrier dynamics. The recombination of charge carriers before they participate in redox reactions can compromise the system's overall efficiency.
iv.
Translating lab–scale successes of Z–scheme heterojunctions to larger scales necessary for commercial or industrial applications can be challenging due to potential losses in efficiency or performance when scaled up.

In the case of S–scheme heterojunctions, Like Z–scheme systems, designing S–scheme heterojunctions require a careful selection of materials that complement each other's band structures. Not all semiconductor materials can be easily matched to form efficient S–scheme heterojunctions; other drawbacks of S–scheme heterojunctions are stated as follows:
i.
The process of fabricating S–scheme heterojunctions can be intricate and may require advanced techniques to ensure proper alignment and contact between the two semiconductors involved. These fabrication challenges can increase costs and complexity.
ii.
Understanding the charge transfer processes within S–scheme heterojunctions is critical for improving their efficiency. However, various factors (i.e., interface structure, defect states, electronic band structure etc.) can be involved in the mechanism of electrons–holes migration, making it challenging to study and optimize the favourable conditions of the photocatalysis process.
iii.
Although the S–scheme heterojunctions are designed to reduce charge recombination, minimizing recombination losses is still a challenge that needs to be addressed for optimal performance.
iv.
It important to note that efficiency of S–scheme heterojunction photocatalysts is highly dependent on how effectively they can utilize solar light. In some cases, if the S–scheme heterojunctions absorb in overlapping spectral regions or if light absorption is not enough, the photocatalytic performance of S–scheme heterojunction photocatalysts can be significantly reduced.
v.
The long–term stability of the S–scheme heterojunctions photocatalysts under continuous operation is crucial for their practical applications for new photocatalysts synthesis, particularly their resistance to photo–corrosion and material degradation over time.

Despite their benefits and limitations, Z/S–scheme heterojunctions are promising research areas, mainly due to their potential to offer efficient and stable photocatalytic performance [95,132–135]. Utilizing CQDs as organic feedstock, it is expected that many of these limitations will be overcome through material innovation and effective synthesis methods. Besides, the development of new strategies to overcome these limitations by exploring new CQDs materials, optimizing the interface engineering, improving the light absorption capabilities, and developing cost–effective and scalable synthesis methods for CQDs–based photocatalysts synthesis. Consequently, Z/S–scheme heterojunctions can offer a good platform to enhance the performance and practical viability of CQDs for a wide range of pharmaceutical contaminants degradation employing the photocatalysis process. Additionally, industrial application of photocatalysis strategy may be undesirable, due to the long duration of the process for effective degradation of pharmaceutical pollutants contaminants and considerable application of photocatalyst dosage. Hence, combination of Z/S–scheme heterojunctions with CQDs materials can improve the detoxification efficiency of pharmaceutical pollutants. Interestingly, CQDs decoration with Z/S–scheme heterojunctions generate considerable contents of radicals (•OH, •O2 –, h+, etc.) via water molecules decomposition, leading to the reduction of the reaction time and enhancing the pharmaceutical pollutants degradation efficiency.

Even though CQDs decorated Z– and S–scheme heterojunctions have shown good efficiency in wastewater–containing pharmaceutical pollutants remediation, it is important to note that various factors such as scalability, stability, and regulatory frameworks influence the commercial adoption and impact on the economy of CQDs–based photocatalysts, the economic aspects of CQDs modified Z– and S–scheme heterojunctions are stated below:
i.
One of the advantages of CQDs–based photocatalysts is their relatively low production cost. They can be synthesized from inexpensive precursors, such as carbon sources (e.g., food waste, fruits, vegetables, biomass, chemicals, flowers, etc.). The low–cost production of CQDs–based photocatalysts makes them attractive feedstocks for pharmaceutical pollutants degradation.
ii.
The production of CQDs from waste materials not only provides an economical solution for waste management but also reduces the environmental impact associated with traditional waste disposal methods and offers a good platform for efficient photocatalytic materials synthesis.
iii.
Their unique properties of CQDs, such as excellent optical properties, high stability, and biocompatibility, make them suitable for various reactive photocatalysts implication in organic pollutants degradation which can drive economic growth in wastewater treatment purposes adopting these materials.
iv.
The incorporation of Z– and S–scheme heterojunctions into CQDs backbone can enhance their performance and efficiency towards pharmaceutical contaminants degradation which can lead to the development of new and efficient photocatalytic feedstocks with economic implications in wastewater–containing pharmaceutical pollutants degradation.
v.
The research and development of novel CQDs–based Z/S–heterojunctions should not only contribute to scientific knowledge but also collaborating with industries in the field of wastewater–containing persistent pharmaceutical pollutants treatment.
vi.
Z– and S–scheme heterojunctions are widely developed to enhance the photocatalytic efficiency of raw CQDs by leveraging the synergistic effects of CQDs derivatives at the junction interface. This enhanced efficiency can lead to a reduction in the amount of the utilized photocatalysts for different pharmaceutical contaminants degradation, potentially lowering the overall cost of their degradation employing the photocatalysis process.
vii.
Z– and S–scheme heterojunctions photocatalysts can exhibit enhanced selectivity towards various pharmaceutical pollutants. This enables more efficient application of reactants and reduces the formation of unwanted by–products. Improved selectivity of Z– and S–scheme heterojunctions can contribute to cost savings by minimizing the toxicity of degraded products of pharmaceutical pollutants.
viii.
Z– and S–scheme heterojunctions on CQDs backbone are often engineered to exhibit enhanced stability and resistance to deactivation and ease generation of the utilized photocatalytic materials in wastewater–containing pharmaceutical pollutants remediation.
ix.
The economic feasibility of Z– and S–scheme heterojunctions catalysts can also depend on the availability and cost of the constituent materials. Careful consideration of the materials employed and their abundance in the market can impact the cost–effectiveness of large–scale production and application of these heterojunctions.
x.
The economic viability of Z– and S–scheme heterojunctions catalysts also depend on their integration into existing industrial processes. Compatibility issues, retrofitting costs, and the overall impact on process economics need to be considered when implementing CQDs decorated Z– and S–scheme heterojunctions in wastewater–containing pharmaceutical pollutants remediation.
Conclusions and outlook

This review highlights the feasibility of CQDs–based Z/S–scheme heterojunctions for wastewater–containing pharmaceutical contaminants treatment. Initially, examples of pharmaceutical ingredients were discussed succeeding by the focus on their impacts on water and human health. Afterwards, different sources of CQDs natural feedstocks were discussed. Exploring novel synthetic methods (i.e., hydrothermal, microwave irradiation, etc.), design strategies, and the characterization of CQDs–based Z/S–scheme heterojunctions were described. The implementation of CQDs–based materials in the photocatalysis process for pharmaceutical pollutants degradation requires also the introduction of appropriate efficient Z/S–scheme heterojunctions on CQDs surface. Furthermore, physiochemical properties of the studied CQDs–based Z/S–scheme heterojunctions were well confirmed by several techniques of analysis (i.e., FTIR, XRD, SEM, TEM, EDS mapping, Ramon spectra, HR–TEM, XPS, etc.). The efficiency of CQDS–based Z/S–scheme heterojunctions has been found due to the effect of the functional Z/S–scheme heterojunctions immobilized on the surface of the CQDs as organic feedstocks employing several preparation methods. Then, the benefits and limitations of CQDs their selves and their decoration by Z/S–scheme heterojunctions for pharmaceutical contaminants degradation were outlined. In the future, the strategies for synthesizing CQDs nanomaterials should be improved, potentially reducing costs and allowing for the production of CQDs with tailored properties for efficient pharmaceutical pollutants degradation using the photocatalysis process. Furthermore, it is worth paying attention to the toxicity and long–term environmental impact of CQDs on the environment, especially as these materials become more integrated into the photocatalysis process as highly efficient photocatalysts for pharmaceutical ingredients degradation. It is important to note that CQDs can be functionalized Z/S–scheme heterojunctions to create sensors for a wide range of pharmaceutical substances degradation thanks to their excellent biocompatibility, low–toxicity, ease of functionalization, high surface area, strong photoluminescence, and chemical stability. Importantly, efforts to commercialize CQDs nanomaterials should be increased in the future, with more companies taking an interest in producing them at a larger scale and incorporating them into commercial products such as efficient photocatalytic materials for pharmaceutical pollutants degradation. It is important to note that the future of CQDs nanomaterials appears to be quite promising with their potential applications in several fields especially in the photocatalysis process, driving further advances in technology and contributing to various pharmaceutical substances degradation. To sum up, this review suggests that the surface of CQDs feedstocks can be easily modified with Z/S–scheme heterojunctions to give efficient photocatalytic materials being utilized and further developed within practical pharmaceutical contaminants photodegradation technologies at present and in the near future.
